text: fat is 0.4g. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, low downside and high payoff is what you want.
carbs is 3.6g. starch is doing most of the heavy lifting here. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
protein is 2.9g. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
vitamin k at 402% DV, vitamin a at 56% DV, vitamin c at 31% DV stand out most. that is major vitamin support.
potassium at 12% DV, magnesium at 19% DV, iron at 15% DV stand out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

on the plus side. elite micronutrient density because this is exactly the kind of nutrient-packed vegetable the category is supposed to reward. very light for the volume because a practical plus for a maintenance-and-repair food. easy to use in lots of meals because works raw, cooked, blended, and as a side or add-in.

now the downsides. contains anti-nutrients because oxalates can reduce mineral usefulness in some contexts. volume shrinks a lot when cooked because can make portions feel smaller than expected. not useful as a fuel food because that is fine for vegetables, but it does limit some use cases.
S tier. Best when you want low downside and easy micronutrient support.
